subject: Chemistry
solution:
(a) Molar mass of Ba $\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2(\mathrm{~s})=221 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ 1 mole of $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2(\mathrm{~s})$ will give 3 moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$ hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{221 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles of $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{N}_3\right)_2(\mathrm{~s})$ will give $3 \times \frac{1}{221}$ $=0.014$ moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$
(b) Molar mass of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7=252 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. 1 mole of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ will give 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$ hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{252 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ will give $1 \times \frac{1}{252}=0.0039$ moles of $\mathrm{N}_2$
(c) Molar mass of $\mathrm{NH}_3=17 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. 2 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ will give 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$
hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{17 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ will give
$$
\frac{1}{2 \times 17}=0.0297 \text { moles of } \mathrm{N}_2 \text {. }
$$
(d) Molar mass of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3=80 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$. 1 mole of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ will give 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$ hence $\frac{1 \mathrm{~g}}{80 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}$ moles $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ will give
$$
1 \times \frac{1}{80}=0.0125 \text { moles of } \mathrm{N}_2
$$
Hence Thermal decomposition of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ will produce maximum amount of $\mathrm{N}_2$.

subject: Chemistry
solution: Gay-Lussac's Law proposes a relationship between the combining volumes of gases with respect to the reactants and gaseous products. According to this law, when gases react together to produce gaseous products, the volumes of reactants and products bear a simple whole number ratio with each other, provided volumes are measured at same temperature and pressure.

subject: Chemistry
options: $11.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of HCl consumed.
$33.6 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is produced regardless of temperature and pressure for every mole of Al that reacts.
$67.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of Al that reacts.
solution:
Reaction: $2\text{Al}(s) + 6\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{Al}^{3+}(aq) + 6\text{Cl}^-(aq) + 3\text{H}_2(g)$
Checking each option:
(1) Per 1 mole HCl: From stoichiometry, 6 mol HCl produces 3 mol H₂, so 1 mol HCl produces 0.5 mol H₂ = 11.2 L at STP. TRUE
(2) Per 1 mole Al: 2 mol Al produces 3 mol H₂, so 1 mol Al produces 1.5 mol H₂ = 33.6 L at STP only, not regardless of T and P. FALSE
(3) Volume ratio at same T,P: 6 mol HCl : 3 mol H₂ = 2:1, so 12 L HCl : 6 L H₂. TRUE
(4) Per 1 mole Al: 1.5 mol H₂ = 33.6 L, not 67.2 L. FALSE
Option (1) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Consider one mole of the oxide, then

Moles of M = 0.98;     Moles of O2- = 1

Let 'x' moles of M are in +3 oxidation state, then Moles of M2+ = 0.98-x

On doing charge balancing we get 

(0.98 - x)2 + 3x  = 2

Or 1.96 - 2x + 3x - 2 = 0

Or x = 0.04

 ∴ %of M3+=0.040.98×100=4.08%

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Mass of $H_2SO_4$ solution $= 50 \times 1.3 = 65\text{ g}$

Mass of pure $H_2SO_4 = 65 \times \dfrac{50}{100} = 32.5\text{ g}$

Moles of $H_2SO_4 = \dfrac{32.5}{98} \approx 0.3316\text{ mol}$

Moles of $Zn = \dfrac{20}{65} \approx 0.3077\text{ mol}$

The balanced chemical equation is:
$Zn + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow ZnSO_4 + H_2$

Since the moles of $Zn$ are less than the moles of $H_2SO_4$, $Zn$ is the limiting reagent.

Moles of $H_2$ produced $=$ Moles of $Zn = \dfrac{20}{65}\text{ mol}$

Volume of $H_2$ gas at STP $= \dfrac{20}{65} \times 22.4\text{ L} = 6.892\text{ L}$

Answer: $6.892\text{ L}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Molar mass of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $2 \times 108 + 12 + 3 \times 16 = 276$ g mol$^{-1}$

Number of moles of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $\dfrac{2.76}{276} = 0.01$ mol

The decomposition reaction of silver carbonate on heating is:

$Ag_2CO_3(s) \rightarrow 2Ag(s) + CO_2(g) + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2(g)$

From the stoichiometry of the reaction, $1$ mole of $Ag_2CO_3$ gives $2$ moles of Ag as residue.

Number of moles of Ag formed = $2 \times 0.01 = 0.02$ mol

Mass of Ag residue = $0.02 \times 108 = 2.16$ g

Answer: $2.16$ g

Zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid to give hydrogen and zinc chloride.

Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl2 + H2↑

The molar mass of Zn is 65.4 g mol-1

Molar volume od H2 at STP = 22.7 L

Number of moles of Zn =11.565.4= Number of moles of  H2evolved 

Volume of H2 = 11.565.4 × 22.7 L = 3.99 L ≈ 4 L

$\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2+2 \mathrm{HCI} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Applying Molarity equation, $\frac{\mathrm{M}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1}{\frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right)}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2}{2}$ or $25 \times \mathrm{M}_1=\frac{0.1 \times 35}{2} \therefore \mathrm{M}_1=\frac{0.1 \times 35}{2 \times 25}=\frac{0.7}{10}=0.07$

solution:
NH2CONH2 + 2NaOH →2NH3 +Na2CO3

By, stoichiometry, 1 mol of urea gives 2 mol NH3

Moles of urea ==0.6 gm60 gm/mol=0.01 mol

So, 0.01 mole urea produce 0.02 mole NH3

To neutralize ammonia HCl is required

NH3 +HCl→ NH4Cl

By equation, it is clear that 1 mol of ammonia is neutralized by 1mol of HCl.

So, 0.02 mol of ammonia neutralized by 0.02 mol of HCl

∴Mole of HCl=0.02 mole

Equivalent of HCl=normality×volume=0.2 N×100 mL=0.02 equivalent

As n-factor of HCl is 1, so number of equivalents=number of moles=0.02 moles

Hence, option 3 is correct.
